Cutoff predictor v2

Two views: Ranked recommends the best college+branch pairs for your profile based on quality, branch demand, and admission probability. All matches shows every reachable option.

Source: TGEAPCET-2025 Last Rank Statements (Phase 1, Phase 2, Final Phase) from official TGCHE PDFs.
The data excludes special reservations (PH, NCC, Sports, CAP, minority quotas) and spot admissions per the source disclaimer. Girls are eligible for Boys seats (TGCHE rule #6) — when Gender=Girls, the tool checks the better of the two columns. 2025 cutoffs are an imperfect predictor of 2026; treat results as informed guidance, not guarantees.
How the ranking works
The composite score is score = w_q · quality + w_b · branch_premium + w_s · admission_prob, with three components on a 0–100 scale:

Quality — percentile rank of the college based on its median OC_BOYS final-phase cutoff across all branches. Lower median = harder to get in = higher quality. Pure data-driven, no curated tiers.

Branch premium — how far below the college's own median the branch's cutoff sits. CSE at CBIT closes at 2053 while CBIT's median is 5246 → premium = +3193 → highly demanded branch. Mapped to 0–100 against the most premium branch in the dataset.

Admission probability — combines (a) margin between your rank and the closing rank, and (b) the cutoff trajectory across the three phases. The margin score peaks at ~10K buffer (you want some safety but no need for 50K). Beyond 10K, a gentle penalty applies for *over-qualifying* — applying to a college whose cutoff is 3x worse than what you could reach is rank waste. A seat whose cutoff is rising sharply (P1 « P2 « FP) is moving away year-on-year, so 2026 may be tighter; this also discounts the score.

Safety bands: SAFE = margin ≥ 15,000 · MODERATE = 5,000–15,000 · STRETCH = 0–5,000. These thresholds are heuristic, not statistically derived from multi-year drift.

Trajectory arrows: ↑ = cutoff rising (competition tightening) · → = stable · ↓ = cutoff falling (competition loosening). Computed as percent change from Phase 1 to Final Phase.

What this doesn't model: NIRF rank, placement data, faculty quality, infrastructure, alumni network. Quality here is purely a *selectivity* proxy — it correlates with reputation but isn't identical to it. Two colleges with similar cutoffs may differ substantially in placement outcomes; verify independently.
